Title: Trump speech to focus on election 'integrity'
Article:
Trump speech to focus on election 'integrity'
Washington, United States, July 17, 2026 (AFP) -
US President Donald Trump will deliver a primetime address Thursday featuring major revelations about election security, the White House said, as he doubles down on his false claims that he won the 2020 vote.
Trump has promised "big news" on a topic that he has revived ahead of crucial midterm elections in November in which his Republican Party could lose control of Congress.
But there is widespread skepticism about what Trump is likely to reveal, while his obsession with the election he lost to Joe Biden six years ago could turn off voters more worried about the Iran war and the economy.
Several major US networks will not break into their evening broadcasts for the speech. ABC confirmed to AFP that they would not do so, while NBC and CNN were also reportedly due to skip it.
White House Press Secretary Karoline Leavitt insisted Trump would "shock" viewers in the speech airing at 9:00 pm (0100 GMT Friday).
"President Trump will deliver a major address to the nation on protecting the integrity of our elections, and we encourage every American to tune in," Leavitt told reporters.
She added it was "very possible" Trump would mention Iran and the economy at the start of his speech.
White House Communications Director Steven Cheung called NBC and ABC "cowards" for not running the address.
Democrats allege that Trump's claims about election fraud are an effort to tilt the scale ahead of the midterms.
More than 60 lawsuits by Trump and his allies produced no ruling establishing fraud capable of changing the outcome in 2020, while recounts, audits and his own Justice Department found none.
- Third impeachment? -
CBS News reported that part of Trump's speech would touch on previously unreported allegations of Chinese meddling in US elections, including that it had compromised US voter data.
It said he would also raise claims that the Central Intelligence Agency knew about the Chinese actions and failed to share the information with Trump during his first term as president.
The heads of the CIA, FBI, Office of the Director of National Intelligence and Department of Homeland Security have been invited to be present while Trump gives the speech, CBS said.
The billionaire president has been pushing Congress to pass a major package of fresh voting restrictions called the SAVE America Act, but there is little appetite even in his own party.
The act would require proof of citizenship to register to vote – which is already required by law in federal and state elections – and photo identification at polling places, while imposing new limits on mail-in ballots.
Trump has never accepted his election loss to Democrat Biden. Months later, he whipped up supporters who stormed the US Capitol on January 6, 2021.
Trump could face a third impeachment trial if Democrats seize control of the House of Representatives in the midterms. He was impeached twice in his first term – including for alleged incitement of the January 6 riots.
His last major televised address to the nation was on April 1, when he gave his first full public justification of the Iran war more than a month after the US-Israeli military campaign began.
dk/mlm
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Ringgit opens higher against major currencies, flat vs US dollar
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The ringgit opened higher against most major currencies but was flat against the US dollar on Friday, as investors remained cautious ahead of the release of Malaysia's advance estimate for the second quarter (Q2) gross domestic product (GDP) today, an analyst said.
At 8am, the local currency traded at 4.0710/0780 against the greenback.
Bank Muamalat Malaysia Bhd chief economist Dr Mohd Afzanizam Abdul Rashid said the market is focused on Malaysia's advance estimate for Q2 2026 GDP growth, with the consensus forecast at 5.3 per cent.
"We project the advance GDP estimate for Q2 2026 to come in at 5.1 per cent, supported by resilient external demand, particularly stronger exports related to the electrical and electronics sector.
"GDP growth of above 5 per cent is considered a solid performance for the Malaysian economy, particularly when driven by investment activity. This should provide positive support for the ringgit," he told Bernama.
Meanwhile, Mohd Afzanizam said the US Dollar Index (DXY) rose 0.28 per cent to 100.763 points as some US Federal Reserve (Fed) officials continue to support higher interest rates to bring down the inflation rate towards the two per cent target.
Therefore, the ringgit will continue to range between RM4.06 and RM4.08 as sentiment continues to revolve around the Fed's interest-rate decision.
At the opening, the ringgit strengthened versus the British pound to 5.4865/4959 from 5.4987/5034 at Thursday's close, rose vis-a-vis the euro to 4.6593/6673 from 4.6678/6718 previously and edged higher against the Japanese yen at 2.5068/5112, compared with 2.5102/5125 yesterday.
However, the local note appreciated versus the Singapore dollar to 3.1551/1610 from 3.1583/1612 at the close on Thursday, and gained vis-a-vis the Thai baht to 12.1161/1430 from 12.1247/1399 previously.
The ringgit was little changed against the Indonesian rupiah at 226.3/226.8 from 226.3/226.6 at Thursday's close, and against the Philippine peso at 6.60/6.62 compared with 6.60/6.61 previously.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Singapore's June exports rise by 20.7pct, lower than expected
Article:
SINGAPORE: Singapore's non-oil domestic exports rose by 20.7 per cent in June from a year earlier, government data showed on Friday, as electronics exports continued to surge on artificial intelligence-related demand while non-electronics declined.
The figure was lower than the median growth forecast of 30 per cent in a Reuters poll, and follows an annual increase of 38.4 per cent in May, which was the largest rise in exports in 20 years. Shipments rose to all of Singapore's top 10 markets, led by exports to Taiwan, the United States and South Korea, Enterprise Singapore said in a statement.
Electronics exports to Taiwan rose by 278.2 per cent on an annual basis, and those to the US rose by 228.9 per cent, the data showed.
On Tuesday, preliminary data showed that the economy grew by 5.7 per cent in the second quarter from a year earlier.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Driver killed, pregnant wife injured in two-trailer crash
Article:
SUNGAI BESAR: A 21-year-old trailer driver was killed, while his seven-month pregnant wife was injured after their vehicle collided with another trailer near Kampung Tebuk Marso here early today.
Selangor Fire and Rescue Department assistant operations director Ashrul Riezal Asbar said the department received a distress call regarding the incident at 2.49am.
He said a team of six personnel from the Sungai Besar Fire and Rescue station was dispatched to the scene.
"According to preliminary reports, the accident involved a Hino trailer carrying 36 tonnes of iron and a Volvo trailer transporting a container," he said in a statement.
He added that the passenger of the Volvo trailer, a 26-year-old woman, sustained light injuries and was rescued by members of the public before firemen arrived.
"The driver of the Volvo trailer was pinned to his seat and unconscious," he said, adding that the driver of the Hino trailer escaped uninjured.
Ashrul Riezal said operations to extricate the pinned victim were carried out  immediately.
"The driver was extricated at 4.15am but was pronounced dead by the Health Ministry. His wife has been sent to the hospital," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Thailand to suspend nearly 6,000 officials in massive exam cheating scandal
Article:
BANGKOK: Thousands of Thai civil servants face suspension over an exam cheating scandal, authorities said Thursday, as investigators widened a probe that has already led to three arrests.
Corruption and bribery cases regularly make headlines in Thailand, but it is rare for so many public officials to be affected.
The scandal began in June, when investigators said they had concluded that officials accepted bribes of up to 800,000 baht (US$23,847) to electronically alter candidates' results to allow them to pass mandatory exams for government appointments or promotions.
Police found irregularities in the results of 5,814 civil servants who had taken these exams, interior ministry official Unsit Sampuntharat told reporters on Thursday.
He said they could be suspended pending a committee decision as early as Friday.
"The damage is enormous, and it is unfair to those who obtained their positions honestly," Unsit said.
The ministry said it would review about 800,000 exam papers as investigators seek to determine the full extent of the alleged fraud.
The alleged masterminds, two men and one woman, have been arrested on charges including destroying and concealing official documents, with one of them apprehended after fleeing to neighbouring Laos, police said.
If convicted, they could be fined and face up to five years in prison.
Prime Minister Anutin Charnvirakul, who has made tackling corruption a priority, condemned the alleged fraud as "disgusting."
Speaking on Wednesday, he warned of a "vicious circle" in which officials used corrupt means to obtain positions of power and responsibility, which gave them further opportunities for corruption.
The arrests followed a 17-day joint investigation by police and anti-corruption agencies, during which officers seized computers and examination documents.
Civil servants in Thailand typically earn between about US$530-US$2,300 a month, depending on rank.--AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Dollar firms as US-Iran tensions renew oil price risk
Article:
NEW YORK: The dollar clung to gains against major peers on Thursday, recovering from a near one-month low on expectations that the US economy will remain resilient, the Federal Reserve will hold rates steady this month and oil price swings may calm.
US unemployment benefits filings fell last week, suggesting continued labor market stability, while US retail sales increased marginally in June as lower gasoline prices weighed on receipts at service stations.
The US economy is less exposed to energy shocks than many of its peers, helping attract safe-haven flows into the dollar when oil prices rise, often at the expense of the euro and yen.
"After softer-than-expected consumer price index and producer price index prints pressured the dollar earlier this week, today's retail sales and claims data, alongside further intensification in the Middle East, have provided modest support," said Uto Shinohara, senior investment strategist at Mesirow Currency Management.
"With seasonal and World Cup-related distortions clouding today's data, and geopolitical developments continuing to have a relatively restrained market impact, gains in the dollar, Treasury yields and oil have remained relatively measured so far."
Oil prices fell 0.8 per cent to US$84.29 on Thursday, still close to their highest since mid-June as the escalating US-Iran conflict heightened energy supply concerns after Tehran asked Yemen's Houthis to stand ready to disrupt shipping through the Red Sea.
"Maybe the dollar is finding a safe haven bid," said Marc Chandler, chief market strategist at Bannockburn Capital Markets. "Iran threat has been a longstanding fear since the war began. The US escalation has some limits and Iran is demonstrating it."
The US dollar index, which tracks the currency against six peers, rose 0.31 per cent to 100.76, off its lowest since June 18 but still on track for a weekly decline.
Chances for a Fed hike in July stood at 10 per cent, versus a 45 per cent implied probability at the start of the week. Markets see 48 per cent odds of at least a 25 basis-point increase in September, according to Fed funds futures prices via CME Group.
ECB RATE PATH IN FOCUS
The euro fell 0.23 per cent to US$1.1437, ending its two-day winning streak. Investors are closely monitoring European gas futures, which have risen to their highest levels since March, stoking concerns that higher energy costs could weigh on the euro zone economy and limit further appreciation of the euro.
The European Central Bank is seen as more hawkish than the Fed, with markets betting on two additional rate hikes into 2027 and some economists not ruling out a first move next week.
"Some ECB officials might actually be inclined to push more forcefully for another rate hike," Carsten Brzeski, global head of macro at ING, said, after mentioning the renewed escalation in the Middle East.
Sterling, retreating from a near two-month high, was last trading down 0.52 per cent at US$1.3469, with investors expecting that Britain's incoming prime minister will pick a fiscally conservative finance minister.
The dollar rose 0.13 per cent to 162.39. It hit a multi-decade high at 162.84 early this month, with attention on potential moves by Japan's Government Pension Investment Fund (GPIF) after Finance Minister Satsuki Katayama said last week the government wants a "substantial" increase in domestic asset investment.
Analysts said the GPIF has the greatest capacity among Japanese investors to influence the forex market. GPIF conducts a strategy review every five years and completed its latest one in 2025. However, it can still adjust its holdings within its target allocation bands.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Geoeconomic fragmentation prompts Malaysia to rethink energy security: Experts
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Malaysia's move to study the establishment of a national petroleum reserve stock reflects a broader shift in national policy as rising geoeconomic risks compel countries to place greater emphasis on energy security alongside economic resilience, according to experts.
IPPFA Sdn Bhd director of investment strategy and country economist Mohd Sedek Jantan said the proposal comes at an appropriate time as the global economy enters a new phase of geoeconomic fragmentation, where economic security is becoming as important as economic efficiency.
He said recent developments, ranging from conflicts in West Asia to trade restrictions and technology controls among major powers, have demonstrated that global supply chains can no longer be taken for granted.
"Malaysia is not necessarily late in considering a strategic petroleum reserve. Rather, it now has the opportunity to design a more forward-looking framework that reflects today's geopolitical realities instead of simply replicating models developed after the 1970s oil crisis," he told Bernama.
Prime Minister Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim said recently that the government would study the need and strategies for establishing a national petroleum reserve stock as part of efforts to strengthen the country's energy security and resilience against geopolitical uncertainties and global supply crises.
Mohd Sedek said the study should be viewed as a long-term national risk management exercise rather than merely a response to current geopolitical tensions, noting that future strategic vulnerabilities could extend beyond petroleum to critical minerals, semiconductors, rare earth elements and other strategic resources.
"The framework should, therefore, remain flexible. Today's policy uncertainty may be centred on developments in Washington, but future geoeconomic risks could originate from any country that controls critical supply chains, strategic commodities or global trade routes.
"Malaysia's strategy should be principles-based and adaptable, to safeguard national interests regardless of where the next disruption originates," he added.
Meanwhile, Malaysian geostrategist and senior fellow at the Nusantara Academy for Strategic Research (NASR) Dr Azmi Hassan said the recent conflict in West Asia, particularly involving Iran, underscored the need for Malaysia to establish a national petroleum reserve strategy to ensure uninterrupted fuel supplies during future crises.
He said while Petronas had managed domestic petroleum supplies effectively, Malaysia should not rely solely on the national oil company but instead develop a comprehensive national strategy to strengthen energy security.
"We need a national strategy in this particular case so that we have an uninterrupted supply of petroleum energy even if global supply chains are disrupted," he said.
Azmi said the initiative would complement Malaysia's existing fuel subsidy mechanisms by strengthening supply reliability, enabling the government to better manage domestic fuel availability during periods of prolonged global disruption.
Cyber warfare expert and security and political analyst at the Centre for Media and Information Warfare Studies, Universiti Teknologi Mara, Dr Noor Nirwandy Mat Noordin said establishing a strategic petroleum reserve could also strengthen Malaysia's geopolitical standing within Asean by positioning the country as a regional model for energy security and crisis preparedness.
He said a robust domestic stockpile would enhance Malaysia's ability to respond to regional supply disruptions while reinforcing its role as a strategic maritime and economic hub capable of supporting regional contingency planning and strengthening supply chain resilience.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Wall Street ends lower as chip weakness offsets solid earnings, economic data
Article:
NEW YORK: Chip stocks pulled the Nasdaq and the S&P 500 lower on Thursday as they continued to lead broader market moves despite generally upbeat US economic data and a strong start to second-quarter earnings season.
Among the 11 major sectors in the S&P 500, technology fell 1.8 per cent, with a 4.3 per cent drop in semiconductor stocks weighing heavily on the sector and the market.
Daily swings in chips have increasingly dictated the overall movement of the major US stock indexes, particularly the tech-heavy Nasdaq.
"It comes strictly down to the weight of the chips in the S&P 500," said Paul Nolte, senior wealth advisor & market strategist at Murphy & Sylvest in Elmhurst, Illinois. "Three or four years ago, it was eight per cent, and now it's over 20 per cent. If you look at the rest of the market, it's doing fine."
The weakness in chips, even after chip demand bellwether TSMC posted a 77 per cent jump in quarterly profit, demonstrated the lofty expectations for a sector that has soared by nearly 70 per cent so far this year.
US-listed shares of the chipmaker fell 2.3 per cent. Memory-chip makers were among the biggest laggards, with SanDisk, Western Digital, Seagate Technology, and Intel down between 5.8 per cent and 12.6 per cent.
"This extreme volatility is very disconcerting for the average investor when they see these huge swings in their portfolio value," said Tim Ghriskey, senior portfolio strategist at Ingalls & Snyder in New York. "(But) a number of the non-tech sectors are doing well, so it's a real mix here."
The Dow Jones Industrial Average fell 105.32 points, or 0.20 per cent, to 52,553.32, the S&P 500 lost 38.63 points, or 0.51 per cent, to 7,533.77 and the Nasdaq Composite lost 387.28 points, or 1.47 per cent, to 25,881.95.
The Dow's losses were cushioned in part by a 1.2 per cent gain in UnitedHealth Group after the company beat Wall Street earnings estimates and hiked its 2026 forecast. Healthcare stocks rose 2.2 per cent
United Airlines fell 1.8 per cent as surging oil prices weighed on its forward guidance. GE Aerospace slid 4.1 per cent, even after the company lifted its 2026 profit forecast.
Analysts have set a high bar for second-quarter earnings season. S&P 500 companies, in aggregate, are expected to post year-on-year earnings growth of 24.8 per cent.
Technology earnings alone are seen jumping 65.5 per cent from the year-ago quarter, according to the latest available data from LSEG.
SOLID RETAIL SALES, LOW JOBLESS CLAIMS, WEAK HOUSING DATA
A spate of US economic indicators released on Thursday showed solid core retail sales, a drop in jobless claims and surging manufacturing activity in the Northeast.
Less positive data came from the housing sector, with a bigger than expected drop in pending home sales and souring homebuilder sentiment reflecting high borrowing costs and strained affordability for would-be homebuyers.
The US and Iran extended their barrage of airstrikes, prolonging a week-long escalation that has all but voided last month's truce. But Iran's release of a US citizen suggested a path remains for the two sides to avert the resumption of all-out war.
Declining issues outnumbered advancers by a 1.08-to-1 ratio on the NYSE. There were 351 new highs and 170 new lows on the NYSE.
On the Nasdaq, 1,817 stocks rose and 2,979 fell as declining issues outnumbered advancers by a 1.64-to-1 ratio.
The S&P 500 posted 42 new 52-week highs and 2 new lows while the Nasdaq Composite recorded 197 new highs and 155 new lows.
Volume on US exchanges was 17.19 billion shares, compared with the 21.19 billion average for the full session over the last 20 trading days.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Australia protests Laos charges over 2024 methanol deaths
Article:
SYDNEY: Australia is "bitterly disappointed" Laos is not pursuing stronger charges over a 2024 methanol poisoning that killed six tourists, the government said in a statement Friday.
Foreign Minister Penny Wong said Australia summoned the Laos ambassador and dispatched an envoy to Laos to convey the government's objections and urge justice for the two Australian women killed.
The Australian government did not specify the charges to be laid, but public broadcaster ABC said it understood Laos would press charges that collectively carry penalties of up to one year in jail and a fine equal to Aus$1,600 (RM4,480). — AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Norwegian suspect in Thai child trafficking case dead, say police
Article:
BANGKOK: A Norwegian man accused of financing a Thai boxing camp allegedly used to traffic underage boys for sex died of natural causes before authorities closed in, police said Thursday.
Officers raided the camp in Rayong province, southeast of Bangkok, on July 7, and on the same day arrested its 28-year-old manager at another location.
Police said the manager admitted to trafficking minors for sexual exploitation and procuring boys to provide sexual services, and said they were searching for a Norwegian man in the case.
But a Thai police source told AFP on Thursday that the man, identified under the pseudonym "Mr  Kim", died of an underlying medical condition earlier this year, before the investigation began.
Local media said he was 80 years old.
"However, police will continue the investigation and identify additional suspects involved in this crime," the source said.
Interviews with the boys indicated that Kim had a female secretary who helped arrange sexual services, including oral sex, from boys with troubled backgrounds, police said in a statement on Wednesday.
In exchange, Kim allegedly paid for their living expenses and other amenities.
Under Thai law, those convicted of human trafficking face up to 15 years in prison for each offence, with harsher penalties for crimes involving minors.--AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Guocoland shares to be suspended on July 30
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Guocoland (Malaysia) Bhd (GLM) says that trading of its shares will be suspended on the Main Market of Bursa Malaysia effective July 30 ahead of its delisting.
The exercise is to facilitate the privatisation of the company through a selective capital reduction and capital repayment by its controlling shareholder, GLL (Malaysia) Pte Ltd (GLLM).
"The trading of GLM shares will continue to be suspended until the de-listing of GLM from the official list of Bursa Malaysia following the completion of the privatisation," the company said in a filing to Bursa Malaysia today.
Under the privatisation, all the shareholders of GLM shall be entitled to receive a cash consideration of RM1.10 for each existing GLM share held by the entitled shareholders on the entitlement date.
The settlement of the offer price will be effected within 10 days from the date on which an office copy of the sealed order granted by the High Court of Malaya dated July 13, 2026, confirming the reduction of the issued share capital of GLM, is received.
In February this year, GLM received a privatisation proposal from its controlling shareholder, GLLM.
GLLM is proposing to privatise GuocoLand via a selective capital reduction exercise at a cash consideration of RM1.10 per share. GLM is a property developer and the property arm of Hong Leong Group.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Gamuda advances Perak-Penang water supply project
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: One of Malaysia's largest interstate water infrastructure projects has moved closer to reality after Gamuda Bhd finalised a bulk water supply agreement for the Northern Perak water Supply scheme.
This paves the way for treated water transfers to Penang from 2032.
The supply agreement, signed with Perbadanan Bekalan Air Pulau Pinang (PBAPP), will enable excess treated water from Perak to be supplied to Penang under a 40-year arrangement running until 2072.
The project, developed through a 50:50 joint venture between Gamuda and Perbadanan Kemajuan Negeri Perak (PKNPk), will see excess treated water from Perak sold to Penang under a 40-year agreement running until 2072.
Gamuda previously announced in November 2025 that PKNPk, through their 50:50 joint venture PKNPk-Gamuda JV, had inked an MoU with PBAPP for the sale of excess treated water to the state.
The target date for the commencement of supply of treated water is March 31, 2032 for 40 years ending in 2072. This is for a minimum offtake of 300m litres per day (MLD), with a potential to increase to 500MLD.
Analysts, meanwhile, said Gamuda stood to benefit from two income streams following the supply agreement.
Public Investment Bank Bhd (PublicInvest) said Gamuda could generate construction earnings during the project's development phase, followed by recurring utility income over the 40-year concession once operations commence.
PublicInvest maintained its earnings forecasts for Gamuda, as the agreement is not expected to have a near-term financial impact, although it anticipates positive contributions from the project's development and the eventual water supply concession.
RHB Research said Gamuda would be able to recognise 50 per cent of the recurring revenue generated from the project at the developer level.
It estimated Gamuda's share of recurring revenue at about RM250 million, with a pre-tax profit margin of around 10 per cent in calendar year 2032.
"Additionally, Khazanah Air Perak which is the project owner of the Northern Perak water supply project may appoint Gamuda as the turnkey contractor.
"The total development value for the project was estimated to be RM5 billion back in July 2025. But the award of engineering, procurement, construction and commissioning works related to the project may come at a later stage," RHB Research said.
PublicInvest maintained its "Outperform" call on Gamuda with an unchanged target price of RM5.60, while RHB Research kept its "Buy" call, with a lower target price of RM6.11 from RM6.19.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Galvanising a team under pressure
Article:
EVERY organisation will eventually face a project where the stakes are high, the deadlines are unforgiving, and there is no room for complacency.
These are the moments that define not only the success of the project but also the quality of its leadership. While strategy, planning and resources are essential, the deciding factor is often the ability to galvanise a team into working with purpose, urgency and unity.
People rarely perform at their best because they are told to. They perform because they believe their contribution matters.
A leader's first responsibility during a critical project is therefore to create a compelling sense of purpose. Everyone should understand not only what needs to be done, but why it matters.
When people can see the significance of their work, commitment becomes stronger than compliance.
Urgency should never be confused with panic. A calm leader creates confidence, even when the pressure is immense. Teams quickly absorb the emotional state of their leader.
If leadership becomes anxious or reactive, uncertainty spreads throughout the group. Remaining composed allows people to focus on solving problems rather than worrying about them.
Communication becomes even more important during time-sensitive projects. Long meetings and complicated discussions are often counterproductive.
Instead, communication should be clear, concise and frequent. Team members need regular updates, clear priorities and immediate feedback.
Silence creates assumptions, and assumptions create mistakes.
One of the greatest leadership mistakes is trying to carry the entire burden alone.
Effective leaders delegate responsibility while maintaining accountability. Trusting capable individuals with meaningful ownership encourages faster decisions and stronger engagement.
People are far more invested in outcomes when they feel personally responsible for delivering them.
Momentum is built through small victories. Rather than focusing solely on the final objective, leaders should celebrate milestones along the journey.
Completing one phase successfully provides energy for the next. These incremental achievements reinforce confidence and remind the team that progress is being made, even when the finish line still feels distant.
During demanding periods, distractions must be eliminated. Every task should be measured against one simple question: does it contribute directly to the project's success? If the answer is no, it should be postponed or removed.
Teams perform better when they are protected from unnecessary interruptions and conflicting priorities.
Leaders must also recognise that pressure affects people differently. Some individuals become energised, while others become overwhelmed.
Paying attention to morale is not a luxury; it is a necessity. A brief conversation, a word of encouragement or simple recognition can restore motivation far more effectively than criticism delivered under pressure.
Accountability should remain firm but fair. Expectations must be clear, deadlines visible and responsibilities understood by everyone.
However, accountability is most effective when accompanied by support. Leaders should ask, "What do you need to succeed?" before asking, "Why isn't this finished?"
Removing obstacles often produces better results than applying additional pressure.
Collaboration becomes a powerful advantage during critical projects. Teams should avoid working in isolated silos where information is guarded or delayed.
Encouraging open dialogue allows problems to be identified earlier and solutions to emerge faster. Collective intelligence consistently outperforms individual brilliance when time is limited.
Flexibility is equally important. Even the best plans rarely survive unchanged. New information, unexpected challenges and shifting circumstances require leaders to adapt without losing sight of the objective.
Resilience is demonstrated not by refusing to change course, but by adjusting quickly while maintaining momentum.
Recognition should never wait until the project has concluded. Acknowledging effort throughout the process reminds people that their hard work is seen and appreciated.
Recognition does not always require rewards or public ceremonies. Genuine appreciation delivered sincerely often has the greatest impact on motivation.
Trust forms the foundation of every high-performing team. Trust allows people to speak honestly about risks, admit mistakes early and ask for help without fear. Leaders who punish transparency create silence, and silence can become the greatest threat to a time-sensitive project.
Encouraging openness enables issues to be resolved before they become crises.
Decisiveness also distinguishes effective leaders. While consultation is valuable, endless deliberation wastes precious time. Projects operating under tight deadlines require timely decisions based on the best available information.
Waiting for perfect certainty often results in missed opportunities. Leaders must have the courage to decide, communicate clearly and adjust if circumstances demand it.
Equally important is leading by example. Teams notice everything their leaders do. They observe punctuality, work ethic, attitude and commitment.
A leader who demonstrates discipline, resilience and accountability inspires others to do the same. Credibility is earned through consistent actions rather than inspiring speeches.
Finally, once the project is complete, leaders should take time to reflect with the team.
Successes should be celebrated, lessons should be documented and contributions should be recognised. Reflection transforms experience into organisational knowledge and prepares the team for future challenges.
Every critical project should leave the team stronger, wiser and more confident than before.
Galvanising a team is ultimately about creating belief. It is about replacing uncertainty with clarity, hesitation with confidence and individual effort with collective purpose.
Deadlines may create urgency, but leadership creates commitment. When people trust their leader, understand the mission and believe in one another, extraordinary results become not just possible, but achievable.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Malaysia AI ambitions resilient despite tighter US chip curbs [WATCH]
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Tighter US export controls on advanced artificial intelligence (AI) chips may only pose a limited near-term impact on Malaysia's AI ambitions, with industry players saying stronger governance and compliance will be key to maintaining access to critical technology.
This follow reports that US chipmaker Nvidia has more than halved the number of Asian customers eligible to buy its advanced AI chips after introducing stricter screening procedures in Malaysia, Singapore and Japan.
The enhanced checks are part of Washington's efforts to prevent cutting-edge AI chips from being diverted to China through third countries, despite existing export restrictions.
Many affected companies were neocloud providers specialising in AI training and cloud computing services.
National Tech Association of Malaysia (Pikom) chairman Alex Liew said the tighter controls do not amount to a blanket restriction on Malaysia but will require companies to meet more rigorous compliance standards.
"While this is not a blanket restriction on Malaysia, companies procuring advanced AI chips may face longer procurement timelines, additional due diligence and higher compliance costs," he told Business Times.
However, he said established operators with transparent ownership structures and strong governance practices should be able to manage the new requirements.
"In fact, stricter screening may strengthen confidence in Malaysia as a trusted destination for AI infrastructure," he said.
Liew said projects that rely heavily on the latest AI chips could experience some short-term delays, but he does not believe the tighter controls will derail Malaysia's long-term ambition to become a regional AI and digital infrastructure hub.
He said Malaysia's competitiveness extends beyond access to advanced chips, citing the country's digital infrastructure, connectivity, talent pool and supportive ecosystem as key strengths.
"What is critical is maintaining Malaysia's reputation as a trusted, transparent and well-governed destination for AI investments," he said.
Resilient Amid Tighter Screening
Malaysia Semiconductor Industry Association (MSIA) said Nvidia's latest move reflected the strengthening of customer due diligence across selected Asian markets as part of a broader compliance framework responding to evolving US export control requirements.
MSIA ppresident Datuk Seri Wong Siew Hai said Malaysia had successfully navigated challenges arising from geopolitical tensions, supply chain disruptions, export control measures, tariff uncertainties and changes in international trade policies.
"Throughout this period, the country's semiconductor ecosystem has demonstrated resilience through higher export value, increased foreign investments and remained an integral part of the global semiconductor supply chain," he said.
Wong said companies operating within the AI ecosystem, including data centre operators, cloud service providers and technology firms, would have anticipated higher levels of due diligence and compliance as global regulations continue to evolve.
"This move should therefore be viewed as part of the normal regulatory process to ensure adherence to international export control requirements," he said.
Juwai IQI co-founder and group chief executive officer Kashif Ansari echoed the view, saying the tighter screening was expected as the US administration had become increasingly concerned about advanced AI chips being diverted into China.
"For Malaysia, preventing physical chips from being exported to China will not significantly constrain the AI ecosystem," he said.
Ansari said Nvidia was removing customers it could not verify were keeping the chips in Malaysia, while legitimate data centre operators, cloud providers and technology companies may face some temporary friction during the vetting process.
However, he said a potentially bigger issue was a proposed US law that would restrict Chinese companies from training AI models using advanced chips located in overseas data centres, including those in Malaysia.
"If that law is passed, it might cut the revenue Malaysian neocloud providers have been earning from Chinese customers," he said.
Despite the tighter controls, Ansari said he does not expect Malaysia's ambition to become a regional AI and digital infrastructure hub to be derailed.
He said the country has an opportunity to ensure more of the advanced chips it receives are deployed locally instead of being re-exported.
Ansari added that the potential restrictions on Chinese companies' access to AI computing power in Malaysian data centres would affect only a relatively small segment of the market.
He noted that while Chinese companies account for about 30 per cent of regional hyperscaler demand, only a small portion would likely be affected by the proposed US measures.
"For example, much of Chinese demand in Malaysia is linked to ordinary operations, such as TikTok and e-commerce data storage for non-China users. That sort of usage isn't at issue," he said.
Sustaining Malaysia's AI Ambitions
MSIA's Wong said tighter export controls would introduce additional compliance and procurement requirements for certain advanced AI technologies.
However, he said the country would remain competitive due to its established semiconductor ecosystem, strategic Asean location, neutral policy stance and conducive business environment.
He said global technology companies make long-term investment decisions based on factors including infrastructure readiness, talent availability, regulatory stability, connectivity, intellectual property protection and ecosystem maturity.
To minimise the impact, Wong said Malaysia should provide industry with timely guidance on evolving export control and compliance requirements.
He added that industry players should strengthen internal governance, supply chain traceability and end-user verification processes to meet international standards.
Meanwhile, Liew called for stronger engagement with the US government and technology providers to ensure legitimate investments continue to have access to advanced technologies.
He added that industry players to improve end-user transparency, compliance and traceability, supported by clear government policies and efficient regulatory processes.
Looking ahead, Liew said Malaysia should continue investing in local AI capabilities, semiconductor design, digital talent and AI applications while encouraging technology diversification to build long-term resilience.
Ansari said companies should also strengthen their "know-your-customer" practices to demonstrate compliance with US requirements.
"Get your processes and paperwork in order so you know your customers and can prove compliance," he said.
He added that Malaysia remains well-positioned as a regional data centre hub due to operating costs that are about 30 per cent lower than Singapore's, as well as its availability of land, power, water and international submarine cable connectivity.
On the policy front, Ansari said Malaysia should continue maintaining good relations with both the US and China.
"Our status as a data centre and AI hub depends in part on being friendly with both China and the United States. The government should embrace measures that satisfy both of these large international partners without choosing a side," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: MH17 resets global aviation rulebook
Article:
news@nst.com.my
KUALA LUMPUR: The MH17 tragedy, which claimed the lives of 298 people, happened 12 years ago. However, its impact and the lessons learnt from the disaster continue to resonate today.
The incident, in which a missile struck the Malaysia Airlines passenger aircraft over war-torn Ukraine, fundamentally changed how the global aviation industry manages commercial flights operating near active conflict zones.
Aviation expert Associate Professor Dr Mohd Harridon Mohamed Suffian said the disaster exposed vulnerabilities in route management over high-risk regions, prompting an  overhaul of global safety protocols.
Harridon said before the tragedy,  warnings regarding conflict zones were sent out via Notice to Airmen (Notam) alerts, but these updates lacked strict enforcement.
"For example, during the conflict, the Ukrainian authorities issued Notam A1492/14 prohibiting aircraft from flying below 32,000 feet and, from their perspective, flying above that altitude was considered safe.
"However, this proved to be a misjudgement, as MH17 was flying at 33,000 feet and was still struck by the missile.
"Within this juncture, it can be stipulated that the Notam was not able to comprehensively function as an effective safety measure," he told the
New Straits Times
.
He said  following the tragedy, conflict zones published in Notams now carry much stricter advisories, directing pilots and airlines to totally avoid the involved geographical areas.
He added that cross-industry sharing of information regarding "probable high-risk areas" has also intensified since then.
Harridon said airlines now acknowledge that a safer operations environment builds  customer trust, which  translates into steady revenue and long-term corporate sustainability.
On July 17, 2014, MH17 was shot down over eastern Ukraine, killing all passengers and crew on board.
The aircraft had departed Amsterdam's Schiphol Airport at 12.15pm (local time) and was scheduled to arrive in Kuala Lumpur at 6.10am (Malaysian time) the following day.
Among the passengers were delegates travelling to the 20th International Aids Conference in Melbourne, including former International Aids Society president Joep Lange.
Two-thirds of the passengers were Dutch, while 43 Malaysians also perished. Other nationalities included Australians, Belgians, Canadians, Germans and Indonesians.
Also on board were Dutch senator Willem Witteveen, Australian author Liam Davison and Malaysian actress Shuba Jay.
ENHANCED RISK IDENTIFICATION
Harridon said the incident underscored the importance of vigilance when operating in or near conflict zones.
He said  after the tragedy, the International Civil Aviation Organisation (ICAO)  ordered countries to stay alert to wars and conflicts in certain regions.
ICAO is a specialised United Nations agency tasked with fostering the safe, secure, and orderly development of global civil aviation.
"It was highly encouraged that nations and airlines actuate comprehensive risk assessments of flight paths  to identify regions or areas that are denoted as highly contentious and detrimental to the safety of flights,"  said Harridon.
RISK ASSESSMENT MODELS
Post-MH17, airlines now evaluate routes using advanced safety programmes, where, if a path is flagged as dangerous, it will be dropped and replaced with a safer and more favourable alternative.
"Airlines will measure and gauge the intended flight path using several risk assessment models such as a flight hazard model, the Monte Carlo simulation and others," Harridon said.
"If the intended flight path is considered hazardous, the flight path will be axed and substituted with a more favourable flight path.
"Airlines are also proactive in updating their Risk Models in accordance with current scenarios, political tensions and hazardous assessments."
AIRLINES MUST UPGRADE RISK ASSESSMENTS
Looking to the future, Harridon said  airlines must upgrade their risk assessment models with past and current data to calculate threats accurately.
"Risk assessments are vital in the aviation industry. The crux of aviation is safety where safety is the number one agenda during flights.
"Thus, it is imperative for airlines and aviation organisations to enhance and augment their risk assessment models  to encapsulate historical and current risk scenarios so that accurate depictions of the risks could be calculated.
"This in turn will alleviate the safety index of flights and also reduce any unwarranted costs due to incidents or accidents."
He said several mathematical models, such as probabilistic risk assessment, genetic algorithm, dynamic risk assessment and others, were available to augment the current risk assessment model.
He also suggested that airlines should have their own dedicated department to perpetually calculate flight risk.
The tragedy led to three Russians — Igor Girkin, Sergey Dubinsky and Oleg Pulatov — and  Ukrainian Leonid Kharchenko being charged  in absentia  with murder in 2019 by the Hague District Court.
On Nov 17, 2022, the court sentenced Girkin, Dubinsky and Kharchenko to life imprisonment for their roles in the tragedy. Pulatov was acquitted.
The Dutch government announced that ICAO had concluded that Russia was responsible for the downing of MH17.
[[nid:1490597]]
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: One dead, 24 missing after boat sinks in Indonesia
Article:
JAKARTA: A passenger boat sank off an Indonesian island, killing at least one person and leaving 24 others missing, the local search and rescue agency said Thursday.
An engine failure caused the boat to sink as it sailed near Selayar, a small island south of the larger Sulawesi, said the head of the local search and rescue agency, Muhammad Arif Anwar.
The crew was unable to repair the engine, prompting the skipper to call local authorities requesting evacuation, he told AFP.
The boat was already partially sunken when rescuers reached it on Thursday, according to Arif.
Forty-nine people were rescued, one woman was found dead, and "24 people are still being searched for", said Arif.
The vessel's manifest listed 50 people on board, but further verification revealed there were in fact 74, he added.
It is common in Indonesia for the number of actual passengers on a boat to differ from the manifest.
An Indonesian Navy vessel had been deployed to assist with the search, which is expected to continue into the night, Arif said.
Marine accidents are a regular occurrence in the Southeast Asian archipelago, due in part to lax safety standards and inclement weather.
In January, three Spanish tourists died when their boat sank off eastern Indonesia, and a 10-year-old boy was listed as officially missing after authorities ended the search.--AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Linggi move is risky for PH, says analyst
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Pakatan Harapan's decision to field caretaker Negri Sembilan Menteri Besar Datuk Seri Aminuddin Harun in  Linggi   while placing his political secretary in Sikamat is  risky.
International Islamic University Malaysia political scientist Associate Professor Dr Syaza Shukri said Linggi,  a traditional Barisan Nasional  stronghold, would present a tougher challenge for Aminuddin.
"It can only pay off if Aminuddin's personal brand is strong enough, which he does have as a menter besar," she told the
New Straits Times
yesterday.
Syaza likened Aminuddin's move to PKR president Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim's decision to contest the Tambun parliamentary seat in the 15th General Election.
"Even for Anwar, it was difficult, and I am sure it would be just as difficult, if not more difficult, for Aminuddin.
"But if he wins, it pays off."
Syaza said Aminuddin and PH would need to highlight his development record and legitimacy as the state leader to convince voters in Linggi.
On Sikamat, which Aminuddin has represented for several terms, Syaza said fielding his political secretary there could increase the risk for PH, although the seat remained relatively safer due to Aminuddin's personal connection with voters.
"The question is whether voters see Sikamat as a PH seat or whether they are voting based on Aminuddin's track record."
However, she said appointing his political secretary as the candidate could help PH retain the seat due to the existing personal link between the candidate and Aminuddin.
"The personal link might reduce the risk for PH."
Aminuddin will contest the Linggi seat in the  state election, moving away from his traditional stronghold of Sikamat.
Aminuddin's political secretary, Nor Azman Mohamad, will instead contest the Sikamat seat.
Sikamat has been Aminuddin's seat for four consecutive terms since 2008, first winning it in the 12th General Election.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: US wants to globalise fight against far-left 'terrorism'
Article:
WASHINGTON: The United States on Thursday launched a scathing attack on left-wing extremists, with Secretary of State Marco Rubio calling for global cooperation to fight a "new wave of this old evil."
In opening remarks at a ministerial meeting on "The Resurgence of Political Terrorism," Rubio said that "far-left terrorism" had become a "blind spot" after the scrutiny on jihadist extremism following the Sept 11, 2001 attacks.
"Even today, the very idea that far-left terrorism could be a serious threat is treated as a right-wing fever dream or, worse, as a dangerous fascist conspiracy," he said.
Citing an increase in leftist attacks in Europe and the United States since 2016, Rubio called for boosting cooperation to combat an ideology he described as "a poisonous resentment cloaked in the language of equality and justice."
Rubio – a fierce anti-communist whose parents fled Cuba – said leftist extremism draws its inspiration from a "hatred for civilisation itself."
More than 60 delegations from Europe to Asia joined the event, where US Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent and White House Deputy Chief of Staff Stephen Miller also spoke.
"You are here because your political leaders are being attacked and stabbed and shot in your streets," Rubio told the delegates, "because your businesses have been bombed, because your railways have been sabotaged, because your police officers have been beaten and burned."
- RIGHT-WING TERROR ABSENT -
Rubio and Miller made no mention, however, of right-wing violence which, according to a comprehensive 2025 report by the Centre for Strategic and International Studies, has been a far larger problem in the United States the past 30 years.
The CSIS report acknowledged there has been an increase in left-wing terrorist attacks and plots over the past decade.
But it said "such violence has risen from very low levels and remains much lower than historical levels of violence carried out by right-wing and jihadist attackers."
The administration of US President Donald Trump has targeted Europe in particular, calling it an "incubator of terrorist threats."
Rubio gave several examples of violent acts blamed on leftist groups in Europe, including the sabotage of the French rail network for the opening of the 2024 Olympic Games in Paris and the killing of French far-right activist Quentin Deranque in February.
According to a State Department fact sheet, there were 21 attacks in the European Union attributed to "far-left and anarchist terrorists" in 2024, compared to 24 attacks from jihadists.
- ANTIFA -
The "Antifa" movement, short for "anti-fascist," has also been targeted by the Trump administration, which designated it a "domestic terrorist organisation" last year after the assassination of conservative influencer Charlie Kirk.
However, experts say it is a nebulous movement of left-wing activists that is more of a political ideology than an organised group.
In the United States, the movement gained traction after Trump's first election in 2016, and many conservatives blamed riots and protests in 2020 after the death of George Floyd on anti-fascist protesters.
In his remarks, Miller launched a virulent attack on "leftists," saying they were spreading a "cancer" in society.
"Not one of the people that is demonstrating looks like a normal person," he said. "They're all deformed in some way in their appearance, in their dress, in their mannerism."
Critics accuse Trump himself of inciting violence.
On returning to the White House last year, he pardoned more than 1,000 of his supporters who had stormed the US Capitol on Jan 6, 2021.
Shortly after Thursday's meeting, Rubio announced visa restrictions on members of "far-left terrorist" groups.
Eleven congressional Democrats told Rubio in a letter that the administration's focus on far-left extremism was "troubling" and appeared to stray from previous Republican and Democratic administrations' concerns about how "racially-motivated violent extremism" posed a threat to the United States.
"The State Department has made no indication that it is taking an apolitical and comprehensive approach to countering violent extremism," wrote the lawmakers, led by the House Foreign Affairs Committee's top Democrat, Gregory Meeks. --AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Middle East war reignites as US pounds Iran, Teheran hits Gulf allies
Article:
TEHERAN: The United States struck Iran and Teheran attacked US allies in the Gulf on Thursday as the foes battled over the vital Strait of Hormuz in the renewed Middle East war.
The rekindled fighting came a month after the signing of a preliminary deal that aimed to end the conflict, which broke out in late February with massive US-Israeli strikes on Iran.
US Central Command (CENTCOM) announced it was conducting a new wave of strikes on Thursday evening to "further degrade Iranian military capabilities" – the sixth straight night of attacks by American forces.
Iranian state TV reported two explosions in the city of Bushehr – home to Iran's only civilian nuclear plant – in a "continuation of the American enemy aggression", as well as a series of unattributed blasts in coastal Bandar Abbas.
The official news agency IRNA also said there were "American enemy attacks on areas around Ahvaz."
Teheran had earlier warned it would target infrastructure across the region if US President Donald Trump followed through on a threat to attack power plants and bridges in Iran – though the White House said he remained "open to diplomacy."
Iran's Revolutionary Guards said Thursday that they struck a US airbase in Jordan with ballistic missiles in response to what they described as an American attack near a children's cancer hospital, also near Ahvaz.
Later on, Iranian state media reported strikes on bridges and an airport in the southern part of the Islamic republic, near the Strait of Hormuz, which has been at the heart of the recent fighting and is crucial to global oil and gas flows.
- 'NEVER BACK DOWN' -
Iranian state media said the hospital in Ahvaz, in the southwest, was evacuated following US airstrikes on the area that foreign ministry spokesman Esmaeil Baqaei slammed as "barbaric."
Hani, a 34-year-old teacher from Ahvaz, said the strikes were "very intense", adding: "My hands are shaking. There were at least 11, 12 explosions. My ears are exploding."
US allies in the Gulf meanwhile responded to attacks, with Kuwait saying it intercepted Iranian drones and Bahrain sounding air raid sirens.
A senior Iranian military spokesman called for the US to withdraw from the region, saying "we will never back down over the Strait of Hormuz", state TV reported.
The Strait of Hormuz was briefly reopened after the US-Iran deal in June, but Teheran said last week it would be closed again "until the US ends its aggression."
The United States has also reimposed its blockade of Iran's ports.
On Thursday, the American military said forces had boarded a ship in the Gulf of Oman to "ensure full compliance", adding that three vessels had been redirected since the blockade resumed.
- THREATS TO INFRASTRUCTURE -
Pakistan's foreign office spokesman, Tahir Andrabi, said Islamabad would "continue to encourage all sides to end violence and resume technical-level talks" under the memorandum of understanding it helped mediate last month.
But Iran's top negotiator Mohammad Bagher Ghalibaf has warned that a deal "only has meaning when its clauses are valid and being implemented."
White House Press Secretary Karoline Leavitt told reporters Thursday that Trump would hold Iran "accountable" for going back on its word, but said "he is always open to diplomacy at the very same time."
"They have expressed they still want to make a deal to the president. We're talking to them, but again, the president is not going to allow them to fire on ships in the strait without paying a consequence for that," she said.
Trump previously threatened to hit Iranian power plants and bridges unless Teheran returned to the negotiating table, telling Fox News: ""Next week it gets really bad for them."
On Thursday, the spokesman for Iran's military headquarters said that if the US followed through on its threats, "all infrastructure in the region" would be "crushed."
Since last week, renewed US attacks have killed at least 30 people in Iran, government spokesperson Fatemeh Mohajerani said.--AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Kim meets top China official to honour friendship treaty
Article:
SEOUL: North Korean leader Kim Jong Un has met a top Chinese official and pledged to deepen ties with Beijing, KCNA reported Friday, as the two countries marked the 65th anniversary of a key friendship treaty.
The meeting Thursday follows Chinese President Xi Jinping's rare visit to North Korea last month, when the two leaders vowed to strengthen bilateral relations as tensions rise on the Korean Peninsula and Pyongyang expands its military cooperation with Russia.
Welcoming China's Wang Huning, a Communist Party Politburo Standing Committee member and other delegation members, Kim declared that it was the neighbouring nations' "steadfast policy... to more vigorously develop the traditional friendly and cooperative relations," the state-run KCNA said.
For his part, Wang vowed that "the firm stand of the Chinese party and government placing great importance on (bilateral) friendship will remain unchanged and the firm support for the cause of Korean socialism led by Comrade General Secretary Kim Jong Un will never be changed," according to KCNA.
The trip came as the two countries marked the 65th anniversary of the 1961 mutual defence treaty.
North Korea has expanded security ties with Russia, where Pyongyang has sent soldiers and munitions to assist Moscow's invasion of Ukraine.
Still, China remains North Korea's largest economic partner, accounting for nearly 98 percent of the country's foreign trade in 2024, according to South Korea's Ministry of Economy and Finance.--AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: PN no longer viable with Pas-Bersatu rift, say analysts
Article:
mukhsein.mukhtar@nst.com.my
KUALA LUMPUR: The widening rift between Pas and Bersatu could leave Perikatan Nasional (PN) functioning as a coalition only in name  ahead of the Negri Sembilan election, say political analysts.
Universiti Malaya senior lecturer Dr Mohammad Tawfik Yaakub said Bersatu's decision to contest under its own logo suggested PN might continue to exist  formally but was no longer operating as a coordinated electoral alliance.
He said the move followed stalled PN seat negotiations, even as Pas held direct discussions with Umno without the apparent consent of other coalition partners.
"The use of Bersatu's own logo is not merely symbolic. It shows that Bersatu wants to protect its identity, candidates and areas of influence from being sidelined in a new Pas-Umno alignment," he told the
New Straits Times
.
[[nid:1490577]]
Tawfik said Pas appeared increasingly willing to shape its electoral strategy based on its own interests rather than through collective PN decisions.
He said this could split the opposition to Pakatan Harapan among several centres of power — Umno, Pas, Bersatu and Wawasan — without a common logo, leadership or menteri besar candidate.
"The nomination process will determine whether this is merely a difference in branding or whether it has developed into a genuine political split."
Tawfik said the risk of a split Malay vote would be high if Bersatu, Pas and Umno fielded candidates against one another or campaigned on conflicting messages.
"If Pas and Umno avoid contesting against each other and Pas supporters are prepared to transfer their votes to Umno candidates, the cooperation could strengthen BN in Malay-majority seats.
"However, if Bersatu contests under its own logo, the Malay vote could still be divided between Umno-Pas supporters and Bersatu supporters."
International Islamic University Malaysia political scientist Associate Professor Dr Syaza Shukri said PN in its current form  was no longer viable. "This would affect the strength of the opposition in Negri Sembilan," she added.
[[nid:1490576]]
However, Syaza said a weaker PN would not necessarily hurt the wider opposition if Pas were to reach an electoral understanding with Umno.
She said Bersatu appeared to have limited electoral influence based on recent trends, while the bigger threat to the opposition would be a Pas-Umno clash that split the Malay vote, benefiting  PH.
Syaza said a Pas-Umno arrangement was unlikely to confuse Malay voters as both parties had promoted the same "Malay-Muslim unity" narrative.
However, she said sustaining it after the polls could prove more difficult, particularly if Wawasan were to  also be involved.
"As an electoral strategy, I think it is fine. However, if they come to power together, I think it would be difficult for Pas and Umno to continue working together, especially with Wawasan involved."
Polling for Negri Sembilan is scheduled for Aug 1, with nomination day tomorrow.
In the 2023  polls, PH won 17 of the 36 seats, with BN securing 14 and PN the remaining five.
Of PH's seats, DAP won 11, PKR five and Amanah one.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Negri polls: BN banks on veterans and new faces
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Barisan Nasional's  decision to retain senior leaders while introducing a few new faces reflects a cautious strategy aimed at capitalising on experience while strengthening its prospects in the Negri Sembilan election, political analysts said.
Universiti Malaya sociopolitical analyst Professor Datuk Dr Awang Azman Awang Pawi said the biggest surprise was not the picks themselves, but BN's move to announce only 25 candidates for the 36-seat state assembly.
He said the coalition's decision to leave 11 seats unannounced suggested it was still pursuing a tactical electoral understanding with "trusted partners" rather than contesting on its own.
"Among the major candidates, there were no real surprises. Datuk Seri Mohamad Hasan remains in Rantau, Datuk Seri Jalaluddin Alias defends Pertang, while incumbents such as Datuk Mustapha Nagoor (Palong) and Datuk Mohd Faizal Ramli (Linggi) have been retained," he told the
New Straits Times
.
Awang Azman said BN's decision to field Faizal in Linggi against caretaker Menteri Besar Datuk Seri Aminuddin Harun was particularly notable.
He said it showed BN was not prepared to concede the seat and had instead opted for an experienced grassroots leader in what is expected to be a closely watched contest.
On BN's choice of poster boy, he said Mohamad, better known as Tok Mat, remained the coalition's strongest political asset in the state.
"He is Negri Sembilan BN chairman, BN deputy chairman, the Rantau assemblyman since 2004 and a former menteri besar who has the strongest political branding in the state," he said.
Retaining Mohamad in Rantau, he added, signalled BN's intention to leverage his administrative experience, political track record and image of stability to take on Aminuddin.
However, Awang Azman said Jalaluddin could also feature prominently in BN's campaign, symbolising the current Umno state leadership and its future direction, while Mohamad represents experience and credibility.
International Islamic University Malaysia constitutional expert Datuk Dr Wan Ahmad Fauzi Wan Husain agreed that Mohamad would remain BN's central campaign figure.
He said Mohamad had proven his leadership by helping BN secure victories in previous general elections.
The combination of incumbents and fresh faces could strengthen BN's position, he said,  while its potential cooperation with other parties reflected growing political maturity at the state level.
"I believe this approach aims to offer the best line-up for the new executive councillors and the 11 additional lawmakers.
"This represents a paradigm shift from narrow political party interests to a higher level of service, prioritising the supremacy of the Federal Constitution, the preservation of our indigenous identity in nation-building and the well-being of our country and its people," he said.
Meanwhile, Awang Azman described BN's line-up as "safe but selectively injected with renewal", saying the coalition had retained experienced figures with strong machinery while introducing new candidates.
He said the inclusion of Muhammad Sufian Maradzi, Mohamad Qayyum Abdul Jalil, S. Leza Md Yasin, Siti Nurumairah Hasim and Suhaimizan Bizar injected a degree of renewal into the line-up.
However, he said the changes were not  enough to portray  a genuine new generation.
"No young or professional newcomer immediately stands out as a campaign star.
"The strength of this line-up still lies in BN's brand, its grassroots machinery and Tok Mat's credibility, rather than the individual appeal of the new candidates," he said.
Awang Azman said BN's biggest test would be whether its experienced candidates could retain traditional strongholds while its new faces succeeded in attracting younger voters and support in mixed constituencies.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: 4-year-old needs RM21,500 for pacemaker
Article:
Your browser does not support the audio element.
WONG Yong Cing, 4, has been diagnosed with a ventricular septal defect, where a hole has formed in the wall separating the heart's lower chambers.
Her family urgently needs RM21,500 for the installation of a dual-chamber pacemaker.
Yong Cing, the younger of two siblings, was born with Down syndrome and also diagnosed with a congenital heart condition shortly after birth.
Her parents did not undergo certain prenatal screening tests during the pregnancy due to financial constraints.
"First, the doctor told us she has Down syndrome. Then they found out she had a 4mm hole in her heart and transferred her to Sultan Idris Shah Hospital (HSIS) for treatment," said her father, Wong Kin Neng.
"When she was 2 months old and weighed about 5kg, she underwent surgery to tie the blood vessel (pulmonary artery banding) because she was too small for a major operation.
"We waited for three years before doctors said she was ready for the next operation."
Congenital heart surgery medical officer at HSIS, Dr Hazwan Hairudin, said there was a hole between the right and left ventricles of Yong Cing's heart.
"Her heart rhythm has become irregular due to a heart block after the operation, and she requires the insertion of a pacemaker."
He said the battery-powered pacemaker could provide electrical stimulation to regulate her heartbeat.
"If the pacemaker is not inserted, the heart may stop beating."
Dr Hazwan said the device would last seven to eight years before requiring replacement and Yong Ching would need to use one for the rest of her life.
Kin Neng is a lorry driver and earns about RM3,600 monthly.
"It is not enough to cover all the costs."
He said his biggest hope was for Yong Cing to be able to eat normally without relying on a feeding tube.
Those wishing to help can send cheques made payable to the Media Prima-NSTP Humanitarian Fund to the Group Corporate Communications Department, Media Prima Bhd, Level 1, Anjung Riong, Balai Berita, 31 Jalan Riong, 59100 Bangsar, Kuala Lumpur, with the patient's name written on the reverse side.
Donations can also be made through the Bank Muamalat account number (1401 0008 8427 18).
Contributions to the Media Prima-NSTP Humanitarian Fund are tax-deductible.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: 'Type 2 diabetes detected in individuals as young as 14'
Article:
Your browser does not support the audio element.
TYPE 2 diabetes is now being diagnosed in Malaysians as young as 14 years old, said Health Minister Datuk Seri Dr Dzulkefly Ahmad yesterday.
"Type 2 diabetes is no longer a disease affecting only adults. It is also affecting teenagers and young people," he told the Dewan Rakyat.
Dzulkefly was responding to Datuk Dr Richard Rapu (GPS-Betong), who asked about the latest statistics on non-communicable diseases (NCDs) and the effectiveness of the "War on Sugar" campaign.
He said data from the National Diabetes Registry showed that 1,926 new cases were reported among individuals aged under 30 in 2024 and 1,932 last year.
In the first half of this year, 660 new cases have been recorded.
He said the country was facing a silent pandemic of NCDs, which was claiming about 94,000 lives annually — more than twice the number of deaths recorded during the Covid-19 pandemic.
Dzulkefly said figures from the Health Ministry's National Health Screening Initiative, which screened 347,813 people aged between 18 and 30 last year, painted an equally worrying picture.
Of those screened, 11,751 were found to have elevated blood glucose levels, while 30,170 recorded high blood pressure readings.
Dzulkefly said the findings showed that a significant number of young Malaysians had risk factors for, or undiagnosed, NCDs without realising it.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Fair pay not enough to keep workers satisfied, says report
Article:
Your browser does not support the audio element.
KUALA LUMPUR: Fair pay alone is no longer enough to make employees feel valued, according to Jobstreet's Salary Pulse 2026 report.
The report found that workers are increasingly assessing their wages based on recognition, career growth and workplace support rather than salary benchmarks alone.
While 81 per cent of workers surveyed believed they were paid fairly for their current roles, only 49 per cent were satisfied with their salaries.
The findings highlight a widening gap between perceived pay fairness and overall job satisfaction.
The study, based on responses from 1,010 employed Malaysians aged between 18 and 64, examined employee perceptions of pay, salary increments and compensation discussions.
Jobstreet said the findings reflect a broader shift in how employees define value at work, with many viewing salary as a reflection of recognition, career progression and organisational support rather than simply market rates.
Jobstreet Malaysia managing director Nicholas Lam said employees are increasingly distinguishing between being paid fairly and feeling genuinely valued.
"Competitive salaries will always matter, but they're no longer the only measure people use.
Employees increasingly want their pay to recognise the contribution they make, reflect opportunities for growth and give them confidence they're progressing in their careers," he said in a statement.
The report found that among employees who believed their pay was "about right", fewer than half, or 41 per cent, said they were actually happy with their salaries.
The survey also showed that while employees were willing to make certain compromises for higher pay, they remained reluctant to sacrifice workplace culture and personal values.
About 19 per cent of respondents said they would accept a lower job title in exchange for a 10 per cent salary increase, while 21 per cent would be willing to remain on call outside working hours.
On salary increments, the report found that 56 per cent of Malaysian workers received a pay rise over the past year.
The survey also found that employees placed greater value on performance-based rewards.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Wildfire smoke chokes Toronto, threatens US cities
Article:
Your browser does not support the audio element.
TORONTO'S air quality ranked the worst among major cities globally on Wednesday as wildfire smoke from northwestern Ontario blackened skies and spread into the northeastern United States, prompting health warnings and calls for residents to limit outdoor activities.
Wildfires are raging through sparsely populated areas hundreds of kilometres from here and sending smoke over a wide area, although they are not directly threatening cities.
Environment Canada reported an Air Quality Health Index reading of 10+, classified as "very high risk", for Toronto, while forecasts suggested hazardous conditions could persist till today.
New York City began feeling the effects days before neighbouring New Jersey is scheduled to host the World Cup final on Sunday.
Local authorities issued an alert as air quality reached an unhealthy level and urged residents to reduce strenuous outdoor activity and take extra breaks if they are outside.
The National Weather Service said smoke could linger into the end of the week.
"We probably haven't seen the worst of it yet for New York City. We probably haven't seen the worst of it yet for the Great Lakes and upstate and New England either," said Dan Westervelt, Lamont associate research professor at Columbia University.
IQAir, a Swiss air quality technology company, ranked Toronto as having the worst air quality across the globe, surpassing Kinshasa and Delhi. New York ranked at No. 5.
Fires briefly trapped two small groups of children from Minnesota who were in the Canadian wilderness on a camping trip organised by the YMCA, the 'Minnesota Star Tribune' reported on Wednesday, citing an official.
One of the groups was evacuated by helicopter in coordination with the Canadian military, said the newspaper.
"Both groups have been rescued and are on their way back home to safety," said Minnesota governor Tim Walz on X.
Wildfire smoke from northern Canada has been a common summer occurrence across wide swathes of the US in recent years.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: SpaceX eyeing first Starship test flight since going public
Article:
NEW YORK: SpaceX is set for a test launch of its powerful Starship rocket Thursday, the first since the company went public.
The mammoth rocket is scheduled for blast off during a 90-minute window that opens at 5:45pm local time (2245 GMT). It will be Starship's 13th flight overall.
As with all launches, unfavorable weather or technical issues could cause delays.
The flight's aims will be similar to those of a mostly successful voyage carried out in May, which debuted the latest edition of the powerful Starship, its third generation model.
The goals of that test were essentially to demonstrate the new designs in flight.
It was not without a few glitches, however, including engine issues with the Super Heavy booster that necessitated a crash into the Gulf of Mexico rather than a precision return.
The company said "there have been several modifications to hardware and software to address issues seen on the previous flight."
SpaceX will aspire this time around to execute a launch, ascent, stage separation, boostback burn and landing burn of the booster offshore without hiccups.
The upper stage will deploy 20 Starlink V3 satellites, and the company will test out a relight of a Raptor engine in space, SpaceX said.
It will also test upgrades to Starship's heat shield.
The latest flight comes one month after Elon Musk's SpaceX went public on Wall Street with a record IPO.
The rocket company has rapidly expanded its satellite internet service and voiced lofty ambitions for AI data centers in space.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Trio held over alleged Kajang apartment food thefts
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Police have arrested three people, including a woman, for allegedly stealing food deliveries left at the lobby of an apartment in Kajang.
In a statement, Kajang police said they received a report at 2.27am on July 14 from a security supervisor regarding the theft of food deliveries at the premises where he was stationed.
The suspects, two men and a woman aged between 20 and 30, are unemployed and have previous drug and criminal record. The two male suspects have between six and eight priors.
Police seized a shirt bearing the police logo and markings from the suspects.
Police said no stolen food was found during the arrests, but closed-circuit television (CCTV) footage showed the suspects allegedly committing the thefts on several different dates.
Urine screening tests on two suspects, a man and a woman, returned positive for methamphetamine. They have been remanded until July 18 to assist investigations, while another male suspect was released on police bail.
The case is being investigated under Section 379 of the Penal Code for theft, Section 89(a) of the Police Act 1967 for possessing or wearing police uniforms, badges or equipment without lawful authority, and Section 15(1)(a) of the Dangerous Drugs Act 1952 for drug consumption.
Anyone with information is urged to contact investigating officer Sergeant Amiruddin Abdul at 013-2304156.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Negri Sembilan polls: PN announces 11 candidates, no fights with BN
Article:
SEREMBAN: Perikatan Nasional announced its 11 candidates for the Negri Sembilan election, none of whom will be competing against Barisan Nasional in the Aug 1 election.
The naming of the candidates was made by PN chairman Datuk Seri Dr Ahmad Samsuri Mokhtar.
Pas will be fielding five candidates, while new coalition component Parti Wawasan Negara will be provide four and Gerakan and the Malaysian Indian People's Party one each.
Among the notable names is former Ampangan assemblyman Datuk Dr Mohamad Rafie Ab Malek, who will attempt to wrest the seat back from Pakatan Harapan.
Ridzuan Ahmad, who held the Gemas seat for Bersatu until the state assembly was dissolved, will now hope to defend it on a Wawasan ticket.
Other candidates include Wawasan's Danni Rais (Klawang), Pas' Mohd Fairuz Mohd Isa (Serting), Dr P. Kumar of Pas (Lobak), Wawasan's Datuk Razali Abu Samah (Sikamat), Gerakan's Lee Boon Shian (Bukit Kepayang).
In Mambau, Wawasan is fielding Erik Michael, while Pas' Kamarol Ridzuan Mohd Zain will be the candidate in Paroi.
The other candidates are MIPP's Sathes Kumar Nilameham (Lukut), Pas' Abdul Fatah Zakaria (Bagan Pinang) and Wawasan's Ridzuan Ahmad in Gemas.
